title: Tool to manage persistent patches to vendored Gambit in Gerbil projects
description: When Gerbil vendors Gambit as a git submodule (src/gambit/), patches to C source files like lib/mem.c are silently reverted by `./configure` which runs `git checkout` on the submodule. A tool that detects pending patches, verifies they survive configure, or applies them post-checkout would prevent repeated loss of fixes. Could also check if a MANIFEST file exists (which skips the git checkout) and warn if it's missing.
use_case: When maintaining custom Gambit runtime patches (e.g. SMP GC fixes) in a Gerbil project fork
example_scenario: Applied two SMP GC fixes to gerbil/src/gambit/lib/mem.c. Running ./configure reverted them via git checkout. Had to reapply patches three times before discovering the MANIFEST guard that prevents the checkout.
estimated_token_reduction: ~200 tokens per occurrence, but saves significant debugging time when patches silently disappear

title: Detect async-signal-unsafe calls in Gambit signal handlers
description: A tool that scans begin-ffi / c-declare blocks for signal handler functions (identified by sigaction/signal installation patterns) and flags any async-signal-unsafe function calls within them. Common dangerous calls: nanosleep, malloc, printf, mutex operations. Only clock_gettime, write, and a few others are safe. This would catch a class of subtle bugs where signal handlers corrupt Gambit runtime state.
use_case: When writing C signal handler wrappers in Gerbil FFI code, especially for SIGVTALRM/SIGALRM interception of Gambit's scheduler
example_scenario: Wrote a SIGVTALRM wrapper that called nanosleep() to throttle Gambit's scheduler spin. The nanosleep corrupted Gambit internal state causing SIGSEGV (exit code -11). Took significant debugging to identify the root cause. A static check would have flagged nanosleep as async-signal-unsafe immediately.
estimated_token_reduction: ~300 tokens per invocation, but saves hours of debugging time for a rare but catastrophic bug class

title: Detect byte I/O on character ports and vice versa
description: A lint rule or compile-check enhancement that detects when byte-level I/O functions (read-u8, read-subu8vector, write-subu8vector, open-input-u8vector) are used with ports created by character-level constructors (open-input-string, open-output-string) and vice versa. Gambit throws "Byte INPUT PORT expected" at runtime but this could be caught statically by tracking port types through the code.
use_case: When refactoring code from character I/O (read-line) to byte I/O (read-u8) and tests still use open-input-string instead of open-input-u8vector
example_scenario: Rewrote LSP transport from read-line to read-u8 for byte-level I/O. All tests failed with "Byte INPUT PORT expected" because test helpers still used open-input-string. The lint tool's existing port-type-mismatch check could be extended to cover this pattern in test files too.
estimated_token_reduction: ~200 tokens per occurrence, eliminates 1-2 debug cycles when switching I/O modes

title: Detect defstruct with keyword-style constructor calls
description: A lint or diagnostic tool that detects when `defstruct` types are being constructed with keyword-style arguments (e.g. `(make-foo field: value)`), which will fail at runtime because `defstruct` creates positional-only constructors. Should suggest changing to `defclass` or converting call sites to positional style. Could also detect the inverse: `defclass` types called with positional args when keywords are expected.
use_case: When writing a new Gerbil project with many struct types and discovering at build time that all constructors fail because defstruct doesn't support keyword args
example_scenario: Created 20+ defstruct types across a project, all with keyword-style constructors like (make-signal-address name: "bob" device-id: 1). Build succeeded but runtime failed with "illegal struct constructor application; arity mismatch". Had to bulk-replace all defstruct with defclass across 32 files. A lint check would have caught this immediately.
estimated_token_reduction: ~2000 tokens per occurrence — eliminates the debug cycle of build, run, discover arity error, investigate defstruct vs defclass difference, then bulk-replace across all files

title: Warn when (export #t) is used in a re-export aggregator module
description: Detect modules that import many sub-modules and use `(export #t)` expecting to re-export all imported symbols. `(export #t)` only exports locally-defined symbols, not imported ones. The tool should suggest `(export (import: :mod1 :mod2 ...))` instead. Could be integrated into gerbil_lint or gerbil_verify.
use_case: When creating a top-level module that re-exports all sub-modules for convenience (like signal.ss that re-exports all protocol modules)
example_scenario: Created src/signal.ss with imports of 15 sub-modules and (export #t), expecting users to just (import :signal/src/signal). At runtime, none of the sub-module symbols were available. Had to discover that (export #t) doesn't re-export and change to (export (import: :mod1 :mod2 ...)).
estimated_token_reduction: ~500 tokens — eliminates the debug cycle of testing imports, discovering they're missing, researching re-export syntax

title: Check Gambit libgambit.a ABI compatibility with compiled modules
description: A tool that compares the struct sizes embedded in compiled Gerbil/Gambit .o modules against the struct sizes in a given libgambit.a or gambit.h. Gambit checks sizeof(___module_struct), sizeof(___global_state_struct), sizeof(___virtual_machine_state_struct), and sizeof(___processor_state_struct) at module load time — mismatches cause ___MODULE_INCOMPATIBILITY_ERR (silent exit code 71). The tool would extract these sizes from both sides and report mismatches before attempting to link, saving hours of debugging.
use_case: When rebuilding libgambit.a with different configure options and relinking into a Gerbil binary. Different --enable-trust-c-tco, --enable-dynamic-clib, --disable-shared, etc. produce different struct layouts.
example_scenario: Rebuilt libgambit.a with --enable-smp but without --enable-trust-c-tco and --enable-dynamic-clib. Copied to Gerbil build dir, ran stage1, binary linked successfully but silently exited with code 71. Took 2+ hours of gdb debugging to discover setup_modules was returning MODULE_INCOMPATIBILITY_ERR due to struct size mismatches between the new libgambit.a and the .o files compiled by the old gsc.
estimated_token_reduction: ~2000 tokens per occurrence, eliminates hours of gdb debugging for a rare but devastating build issue

title: Detect missing function calls between parallel code layers (Qt vs TUI parity)
description: A tool that compares two parallel modules (e.g. Qt app-init! vs TUI app-init!) and detects function calls present in one but missing from the other. In projects with dual rendering layers (like gemacs with Qt and TUI), critical initialization calls like init-gemacs-log! can be present in one layer but accidentally omitted from the other, causing silent failures (no logging, no error messages, features silently broken). The tool would parse both entry points, extract function calls, and report asymmetries.
use_case: When maintaining parallel code paths (Qt GUI + TUI terminal) that must call the same initialization and lifecycle functions
example_scenario: gemacs Qt layer called init-gemacs-log! in qt/app.ss but the TUI layer in app.ss did not. All gemacs-log! calls in the TUI were silently dropped because *gemacs-log-port* was never initialized. Spent 30+ minutes adding debug logging that never appeared, before discovering the missing init call. A parity check tool would have flagged this instantly.
estimated_token_reduction: ~3000 tokens per occurrence — eliminates entire debug cycles where logging appears broken but the real issue is missing initialization

title: Track paren depth at edit boundaries to catch imbalance early
description: When gerbil_balanced_replace or gerbil_check_balance reports "Balance OK", but the file actually has mismatched parens due to multi-function edits (e.g., converting 3 functions from sync to async in one session), errors cascade through the rest of the file. A tool that reports the paren depth at each top-level form boundary would instantly pinpoint which function has the imbalance, rather than requiring manual binary search or python scripts to trace depth changes line by line.
use_case: When making multiple function-level edits (e.g., wrapping bodies in spawn/lambda) and one edit has a missing paren that cascades through the rest of the file
example_scenario: Converted 3 dired functions from sync to async (adding spawn/name + ui-queue-push! wrappers). Each needed 13 closing parens but one had only 12. The balance checker said 'unclosed at line 1270' but couldn't pinpoint which of the 3 functions was wrong. Had to write a python script to trace depth changes per line. A depth-at-boundaries tool would have shown 'depth 1 at line 1269 (should be 0)' immediately.
estimated_token_reduction: ~800 tokens per occurrence — eliminates the need for python depth-tracing scripts and manual binary search

title: Auto-clean stale upstream static artifacts before building
description: Enhance gerbil_build_and_report to automatically detect and remove stale static artifacts from upstream linked packages (gerbil.pkg depend: entries) before starting a build. When a dependency like gerbil-aws is rebuilt with new modules or changed struct definitions, the downstream project (e.g. kunabi) retains old .scm files in .gerbil/lib/static/ that the exe linker uses, causing hangs, broken binaries, or silent use of outdated code. The tool should: (1) check each depend: package's build timestamp vs the project's cached static/ files, (2) auto-remove stale ones, (3) log what was cleaned. This should be the DEFAULT behavior of gerbil_build_and_report, not a separate manual step. The existing gerbil_stale_static and gerbil_stale_linked_pkg tools detect the problem but require the user (or Claude) to remember to run them — which repeatedly fails in practice.
use_case: Every time an upstream dependency is rebuilt and the downstream project is then built, stale artifacts cause the build to hang or produce broken binaries. This happens multiple times per session when iterating on library + consumer projects.
example_scenario: Rebuilt gerbil-aws (added s3/connection.ss, changed s3-client struct). Then ran gerbil_build_and_report on kunabi. The exe linker used stale gerbil-aws__s3__api.scm from kunabi's .gerbil/lib/static/ which referenced the old 5-field s3-client, not the new 6-field version. Build hung indefinitely. Had to manually kill, rm stale files, rebuild. This exact pattern has happened 3+ times in this session alone.
estimated_token_reduction: ~2000-5000 tokens per occurrence (kill stuck build, diagnose, clean, retry). Occurs multiple times per cross-project session.

title: Detect SMP deadlock risk in concurrent I/O code
description: Static analysis tool that scans Gerbil source files for patterns known to deadlock under Gambit SMP: concurrent threads doing SSL I/O via :std/net/request or :std/net/ssl, thread-start! combined with http-get/ssl-connect in loops, and missing -:p1 workarounds. Reports risk level and suggests mitigations (green thread concurrency, -:p1 flag, or restructuring I/O to single thread).
use_case: When writing concurrent network code that will be compiled with SMP-enabled Gambit. Prevents shipping binaries that deadlock under load.
example_scenario: User writes 8 worker threads each calling http-get in a loop. Code compiles fine and passes low-volume tests but deadlocks in production with 1000+ requests. This tool would flag the pattern at development time.
estimated_token_reduction: ~2000 tokens per debugging session (eliminates hours of strace investigation)

title: Expand gambit_primitive_lookup to cover GC and system info primitives
description: The gambit_primitive_lookup tool returns no results for common ## primitives like ##gc, ##process-statistics, ##get-live-percent, ##cpu-cache-size, ##system-version-string, ##system-type-string, ##get-bytes-allocated!, ##configure-command-string. These are widely used for diagnostics and profiling but the tool's search doesn't find them, forcing fallback to gerbil_eval to probe which primitives exist and their calling conventions (e.g. ##get-bytes-allocated! requires f64vector+offset args, not obvious).
use_case: When building diagnostic/profiling features that need Gambit runtime introspection primitives
example_scenario: Needed to find all available GC/heap/system primitives for a ,room command. Searched gambit_primitive_lookup for 'gc', 'heap', 'memory' — all returned empty. Had to use gerbil_eval to probe (with-catch ...) each primitive name individually, then figure out calling conventions by trial and error (##get-bytes-allocated! takes 2 args not 0).
estimated_token_reduction: ~500 tokens per diagnostic task — eliminates 3-4 gerbil_eval probe calls

title: Warn when a module defines a symbol referenced externally but missing from its explicit export list
description: When a module uses an explicit `(export sym1 sym2 ...)` list (not `(export #t)`), newly added function definitions are not automatically exported. Consumers that reference the new symbol get a compile-time "Reference to unbound identifier" error. `gerbil_verify` or `gerbil_lint` should detect this by cross-referencing the module's export list against its definitions and flagging any definition that is called from other modules in the project but not listed in the export. This is the inverse of "exported but not defined" (already checked) — it's "defined but not exported yet referenced externally."
use_case: When adding new functions to a module with an explicit export list and forgetting to add them to the export declaration. The error only surfaces at build time in the consuming module, not at the defining module, making the root cause non-obvious.
example_scenario: Added `notification-push!` and `notification-get-recent` to core.ss (which has a 70-line explicit export list, not `(export #t)`). Build succeeded for core.ss itself but failed in `editor-extra-media2.ss` with "Reference to unbound identifier: notification-get-recent". Had to grep through core.ss's export list, find the echo-message! section, and manually add the 3 new symbols. The error message pointed to the consumer, not the source of the omission.
estimated_token_reduction: ~300 tokens per occurrence — eliminates one build cycle and the grep-through-export-list investigation

title: gerbil_check_balance should suggest gerbil_read_forms when reporting unclosed form
description: When gerbil_check_balance reports an unclosed form at a line number, it should also suggest running gerbil_read_forms for more precise diagnosis. gerbil_read_forms uses the actual Gerbil reader and reports exactly which top-level form number is incomplete and at what line range it started — making it trivial to find the exact function with the paren error. gerbil_check_balance only reports the line where balance goes negative, not which function definition is the culprit.
use_case: When a build fails with 'Incomplete form, EOF reached' and gerbil_check_balance reports the unclosed position but you need to know which function definition is broken.
example_scenario: Build fails on qt/commands-edit2.ss with 'Incomplete form, EOF reached'. gerbil_check_balance says unclosed at line 1615. gerbil_read_forms shows 'Forms [0]-[65] read OK, error at line 1615' — making clear that forms [66] and [67] (cmd-wdired-finish-edit and cmd-wdired-abort) are the broken ones. Without read_forms, you need to manually search near line 1615 for the unclosed function.
estimated_token_reduction: ~300 tokens per balance-debugging session — eliminates the need to call gerbil_read_forms as a separate follow-up step
